title: Data appendix
text:
This page is the empirical backing for the landing page. It is versioned alongside the code that produces the numbers. If a value changes on re-run, the value here changes, and the commit history records why. The scaled chain-on-21k comparison is the one open cell; everything else is measured.
Setup

title: Data appendix
text:
The corpus
The evaluation runs on MuSiQue (Trivedi et al., 2022), a multi-hop question-answering benchmark constructed by chaining single-hop questions from SQuAD, T-REx, Zero Shot RE, Natural Questions, and MLQA. The version used here is the answerable split (2-, 3-, and 4-hop questions with grounded supporting evidence).

Full corpus: 21,100 candidate paragraphs (the union of every question’s supporting passages plus MuSiQue’s distractor pool).
Gold-only subset: 281 chunks, restricted to the paragraphs that contain the supporting evidence for the 120-question stratified sample. This isolates the retrieval-ranking problem from the recall problem.
Held-out test set: 90 questions, 30 each at 2-, 3-, and 4-hop.

Rank distribution

title: Data appendix
section: Where do the supporting chunks land?
text:
Where do the supporting chunks land?
The metric is g_i: the 1-indexed rank, within the corpus’s cosine ranking, of the i-th-closest gold chunk for a question. g_1 is the gold chunk the retriever ranks highest; g_4 is the deepest of a 4-hop question’s four gold chunks. Cells report median [p75] (max) over 30 questions.

Dense RAG on the full 21,100-chunk corpus










hops
g1
g2
g3
g4




2-hop
1 [2] (8)
143 [786] (5042)




3-hop
1 [2] (16)
13 [47] (1138)
101 [522] (5775)



4-hop
1 [3] (129)
45 [182] (1388)
268 [1049] (2394)
1796 [3027] (9572)





Dense RAG on the 281-chunk gold-only corpus



hops
g1
g2
g3
g4




2-hop
1 [1] (1)
3 [17] (79)




3-hop
1 [1] (1)
2 [3] (15)
5 [12] (77)



4-hop
1 [1] (3)
3 [5] (37)
7 [21] (45)
30 [65] (144)



Reading the two tables together makes the shape unmistakable. g_1 sits at rank 1 in both corpora, at every hop count: the chunk that directly matches the question’s named entity is trivial for cosine. The deeper gold chunks are the ones that move. On 281 chunks, g_4 for a 4-hop question sits at median 30, entirely usable. On 21,100 chunks, the same g_4 sinks to median 1,796 with a p75 of 3,027. First hop is not the problem. The problem is every gold chunk after the first, scaled against realistic distractors.


Per-hop coverage at production K
The rank medians turn into a concrete “what does the language model actually see?” table if you count, for each g_i, the fraction of questions where that gold chunk lands inside the top K. This is the useful metric. Numbers below are computed directly from cache/p3v5/vanilla_dense_21k.json, TEST split, 30 questions per hop group.



Hop group
Slot
K = 5
K = 10
K = 30
K = 100




2-hop
g1
93 %
100 %
100 %
100 %


2-hop
g2
10 %
17 %
43 %
43 %


3-hop
g1
93 %
97 %
100 %
100 %


3-hop
g2
20 %
37 %
60 %
87 %


3-hop

0 %
0 %
23 %

4-hop

4-hop

4-hop
g3
0 %
3 %
13 %
37 %


4-hop
g4
0 %
0 %
0 %
7 %



The story reads across the table in two directions. Down each K column: g_1 always survives, and the coverage of every deeper slot collapses. Along each row from left to right: raising K helps the middle slots but does not touch the tail. 4-hop g_4 is a zero at K = 30 and only 7 % at K = 100. That is what “dense at scale cannot rank the deep chunks” means concretely; it is not that they are missing from the corpus, it is that dense sorts them into the thousands.

Why not headline all-gold@K? Because it is a strict-necessity metric (every gold chunk inside K, else the question is a miss) and it hides where the collapse actually happens. “22 % at K = 30” reads as “the retriever is a bit weak” and quietly gets discounted for “surely the LLM can guess with most of the chunks.” The per-hop table above says the same thing without letting the reader off the hook: the deep chunks are nowhere inside K.



Why K = 30 is the useful ceiling
Lost-in-the-middle (Liu et al., 2024) shows a U-shaped attention curve: language models attend well to the start and end of a long context and poorly to the middle. The penalty is measurable across both open-source and commercial models, and it gets worse with longer contexts. K = 30 is roughly the point where the middle-position penalty starts costing more accuracy than the extra recall buys back. Above K = 30 you are trading retriever recall against the model’s ability to use what you retrieved.
K = 10 is the production cutoff most systems settle at because it sits under the U-curve entirely: every chunk is at a position the model attends to well. The modern pattern is retrieve K = 50-100 with a cheap dense/BM25 hybrid, then rerank down to K = 5-10 with a cross-encoder, which lets you use retriever recall without paying the attention penalty. The rerank step helps recover shallow multi-hop chunks (the 2-hop g_2 in the table above) but cannot recover chunks that are outside the initial K = 100 candidate pool. Look at 4-hop g_4 (7 % at K = 100) and 4-hop g_3 (37 % at K = 100). Reranking cannot save what dense retrieval never surfaced.
Comparison

title: Data appendix
text:
Gold-only: dense vs graph-informed
The gold-only subset lets us compare two retrieval strategies without the recall confound. Both strategies see the same 281 paragraphs; the only difference is how they rank them.

Dense: cosine similarity between question embedding and paragraph embeddings (mpnet, L2-normalized), single-shot.
Chain v5c2: bipartite chunk-entity graph with structural occurrence-count edges (no chunk-chunk, no entity-entity). Random walk with restart, c = 0.15, alpha = 0.5, two-channel softmax seeding on question-chunk-text and question-entity-label.












hops
metric
g1
g2
g3
g4




2-hop
dense
1 [1] (1)
3 [17] (79)




2-hop
chain
1 [1] (1)
2 [3] (54)




3-hop
dense
1 [1] (1)
2 [3] (15)
5 [12] (77)



3-hop
chain
1 [1] (2)
2 [2] (24)
4 [5] (71)



4-hop
dense
1 [1] (3)
3 [5] (37)
7 [21] (45)
30 [65] (144)


4-hop
chain
1 [1] (3)
2 [3] (33)
5 [12] (77)
19 [29] (206)



Three things fall out of this table:

g_1 is a tie. Dense cosine trivially finds the seed chunk; the walk adds nothing on the first hop, because the first hop is not the problem.
The chain wins on bridge chunks, more so the deeper the hop. 2-hop g_2 p75 drops from 17 to 3. 4-hop g_4 p75 drops from 65 to 29. The chunks that dense retrieval buries because they do not lexically match the question are the chunks the entity-bridge walk keeps near the top.
One reversal. The chain’s worst-case 4-hop g_4 (max 206) is worse than dense’s (max 144). Walks usually lift deep chunks but occasionally misroute mass and bury a single chunk worse than dense does. This is a known failure mode of RWR, not a metric artifact.

The chain’s advantage concentrates on the deepest hop, which is exactly where the trajectory-length argument predicts it should: the walk’s reach scales with (1 - c) / c, and a low restart probability sends mass further into the graph.
Open

title: Data appendix
text:
The one open comparison
The 21k-scale chain-vs-dense comparison is the still-open cell. Dense RAG on 21k is the collapsed baseline (g_4 median 1,796, all-gold@30 22 %). The chain has been run on the 281 gold-only corpus (g_4 median 19). The chain on the full 21k corpus is deferred, blocked on a distillation step estimated at roughly $300 in compute. The numbers here will be updated as soon as that run lands.
The prediction to falsify: at 21k, the chain keeps every g_i inside K = 30, closing the all-gold@30 gap from 22 % well into the 60s or higher. If it does not, the operator-splitting story on the landing page needs an accounting.
Methodology

title: Data appendix
text:
How the graph is built
Chain v5c2 uses a bipartite chunk-entity graph. Concretely:

Entity extraction over each paragraph, producing a canonical entity label per mention.
Chunk-entity edges weighted by the count of the entity’s occurrences inside the chunk. No chunk-chunk edges. No entity-entity edges.
Two transition temperatures at the walk step: T_entity = 0.05 when transitioning from entity to chunk, T_chunk = 0.05 when transitioning from chunk to entity. Both act as softmax sharpness.
Two-channel seeding at query time: the question is embedded and softmaxed against chunk texts and entity labels separately, then the two seed distributions are mixed with alpha = 0.5.

The walk is a personalized PageRank with restart c = 0.15. Full ranking over all corpus chunks is read off the stationary distribution; no top-K cutoff is applied at ranking time.
For projects that already ship linked notes (a wiki with [[link]] syntax and typed frontmatter), the entity layer is unnecessary because the concept graph is the wiki graph itself. This is the setting where the approach is cheapest to deploy.
Caveats

title: Data appendix
text:
What we are not claiming

This does not replace dense retrieval. Dense similarity seeds the walk. The claim is that walk-informed re-ranking closes the gap on hops dense-alone misses.
The 21k chain result is not in yet. The 21k dense collapse is measured; the 21k chain result is projected, not observed. Do not cite the chain at scale until the deferred run completes.
This has not been evaluated on all corpora. MuSiQue is a stress test built for multi-hop; results here do not automatically generalize to single-hop domains or to corpora where evidence is not graph-structured.
GraphRAG and community-based methods are related. (Edge et al., 2024) Hierarchical community summaries are a different way to solve the same envelope-assembly problem. Which method wins depends on corpus structure; both beat plain dense at scale.

title: Data appendix
text:
Reproduction
# Dense RAG rank positions on the 21k corpus
python -m experiments.musique.sparql.design.vanilla_dense_positions --scope full

# Chain v5c2 rank positions on the 281 gold-only corpus
python -m experiments.musique.chain_v5c2_rank_analysis

title: Data appendix
text:
References


Edge, D., Trinh, H., Cheng, N., Bradley, J., Chao, A., Mody, A., Truitt, S., & Larson, J. (2024). From local to global: A graph RAG approach to query-focused summarization. arXiv Preprint arXiv:2404.16130.


Liu, N. F., Lin, K., Hewitt, J., Paranjape, A., Bevilacqua, M., Petroni, F., & Liang, P. (2024). Lost in the middle: How language models use long contexts. Transactions of the Association for Computational Linguistics, 12, 157–173. https://doi.org/10.1162/tacl_a_00638


Trivedi, H., Balasubramanian, N., Khot, T., & Sabharwal, A. (2022). MuSiQue: Multihop questions via single-hop question composition. Transactions of the Association for Computational Linguistics, 10, 539–554. https://doi.org/10.1162/tacl_a_00475

title: Retrieval as trajectory
text:
The claim
The rest of this page unpacks what that means, why it matters, and what an alternative looks like. The data appendix has the underlying rank tables and methodology.
The failure mode

title: Retrieval as trajectory
text:
Dense RAG at scale
Dense retrieval on a modest corpus, of order a thousand chunks, and a question that needs one chunk to answer, is very good. It is the case most public benchmarks are built around, and it is the case the “just do RAG” advice was written for.
Multi-hop QA at scale is a different problem. MuSiQue (Trivedi et al., 2022) provides a stress test: 21,100 candidate paragraphs, questions that require chaining evidence across two, three, or four documents, and a rank distribution that collapses hop by hop. Concretely, on the MuSiQue TEST set with plain dense retrieval, the rank of each gold chunk:










Hop group
Direct-match chunk
Bridge chunk
Deeper bridge
Deepest chunk




2-hop
median rank 1
143




3-hop
1
13
101



4-hop
1
45
268
1,796



The direct-match chunk lands at rank 1 at every hop count, even against 21,000 distractors. Cosine similarity finds it every time. Every chunk after that scatters. The deepest chunk of a 4-hop question sits at median rank 1,796, p75 3,027, worst case 9,572. Dense retrieval on this corpus is not returning noise; it is returning the wrong specific evidence, which is worse.
This is not a claim about dense retrieval being bad. It is a claim about what dense retrieval is for. It is a one-shot query against a similarity manifold. If the answer lives one hop away in similarity space, it finds it. If the answer lives across a graph that the embedding does not encode, it cannot.

The K question sharpens this
Two constants set the operating envelope:

K = 10 is the modal production cutoff. It keeps latency and cost down and it puts every retrieved chunk in a position the language model actually attends to. This is the number to design against.
K = 30 is the upper bound, the point at which lost-in-the-middle effects (Liu et al., 2024) start eating into answer quality faster than extra recall helps. The U-shaped attention curve means chunks in positions 10 to 20 of a 30-chunk context are systematically harder for the model to use than chunks at the ends. This ceiling matters because it closes the escape hatch of “just retrieve more.”

What the language model actually sees at K = 10 on the 21k corpus, per hop group (fraction of TEST questions whose slot-i gold chunk lands inside the top 10):



Hop group
g1
g2
g3
g4




2-hop
100 %
17 %




3-hop
97 %
37 %
0 %



4-hop
87 %
23 %
3 %
0 %



The direct-match chunk is essentially always in front of the model. Everything else drops off a cliff. For a 4-hop question, the model receives g_1 in 87 % of cases and effectively nothing else; the deepest chunk (g_4) lands inside K = 10 for zero out of thirty questions.
Raising to K = 30 recovers some middle chunks but does not touch the tail: 4-hop g_3 climbs to 13 %, 4-hop g_4 stays at 0 %. Even at K = 100, 4-hop g_4 is only 7 %. This is the shape that matters. The failure is not a coverage bug you can fix by turning K up. It is a ranking failure: the deep chunks are in the corpus, and dense retrieval sorts them far below the direct-match. See the data appendix for the full K = 5 / 10 / 30 / 100 sweep.
On terminology

title: Retrieval as trajectory
text:
Two kinds of “hop”
A lot of confusion in the multi-hop literature is because the word “hop” is doing two different jobs.
Reasoning-hops are a property of the question. How many inference steps does the question demand? “What year was the director of Blade Runner born?” is a two-reasoning-hop question because the model has to resolve a director and then a birth year. This is the sense of “hop” used in MuSiQue and elsewhere in the QA-benchmark tradition.
Retrieval-hops are a property of the index. How many graph edges does the supporting evidence live across? A retrieval-hop is a step across a link in the corpus itself: a citation, a shared concept, a cross-document reference. This is the sense of “hop” used in HopRAG (Zhang & Others, 2024) and IRCoT (Trivedi et al., 2023).
The two are correlated but not the same, and mistaking one for the other leads to the objection we heard most often at talks:

“If the question is 3-hop, why not just decompose it into three 1-hop questions?”
Because question decomposition operates on the question side. It reduces the reasoning-hop count. It does not help you assemble the context envelope, which lives on the index side. If the supporting evidence is spread across a graph, running three separate similarity queries against that graph gets you three separate one-hop retrievals, none of which is the walk-informed neighborhood that would have surfaced the envelope in one shot. Decomposition composes with graph retrieval; it does not replace it.

The mechanism

title: Retrieval as trajectory
text:
Retrieval as a trajectory
The alternative to “one query, top-K by similarity” is “walk the graph.” The graph used in the results below is deliberately austere: two node types, chunks and canonical entities extracted from those chunks, with edges weighted by how often each entity occurs inside each chunk. No chunk-chunk edges, no entity-entity edges. Once the graph exists, a random walk with restart gives you a stationary distribution that captures where information tends to cluster:
\[
\boldsymbol{\pi} = c \cdot \boldsymbol{\pi}_0 + (1 - c) \cdot \mathbf{P}^{\top} \boldsymbol{\pi}
\]
The walker restarts at a seed node with probability \(c\), otherwise it follows an outbound edge, and it repeats until the distribution \(\boldsymbol{\pi}\) stops moving. The result is a personalized-PageRank score over the whole graph, one score per node, telling you how much local neighborhood a node absorbs from the seed.
The animation below runs this. Yellow nodes are the seed set; darker nodes have accumulated more probability mass through the walk; the graph structure controls where the mass ends up.




Live simulation by Priscila Moreira. Refresh the page or click a node to reseed. What matters visually: mass flows along dense sub-graphs, and short paths through concept edges pull mass across document boundaries that similarity search would treat as unrelated.



Why the walk happens before the question arrives
The RWR is not run at query time. The stationary distribution is computed once, offline, and cached. When a question comes in, the retrieval step is:

Use similarity to identify a small seed set of chunks or concepts related to the question.
Read off the pre-computed walk-informed neighborhood around that seed.
Re-rank the neighborhood locally against the question.

This is why “just decompose the question” is not a substitute. The graph structure that the walk discovered is already there. The question is used to choose which walk-informed neighborhood to pull, not to run a fresh walk from scratch. Decomposition helps you seed more precisely; it does not replace the trajectory.
The result

title: Retrieval as trajectory
text:
What this buys you
On the 281-chunk gold-only subset, the chain closes the ranking gap on exactly the chunks dense retrieval buries. g_1 is a tie at rank 1 for both retrievers, as it should be. The 2-hop bridge chunk (g_2) drops from a dense p75 of 17 to a chain p75 of 3. The 4-hop deepest chunk (g_4) drops from a dense p75 of 65 to a chain p75 of 29. Full tables and the metric definition are in the data appendix.
The honest caveats:

The strongest evidence right now is on the gold-only subset, where every gold chunk is guaranteed to be present in the corpus. This isolates the retrieval-ranking problem from the recall problem, and is where the chain has been fully evaluated.
The 21k-scale chain-vs-dense comparison is not run yet. The dense side is measured (g_4 median 1,796). The chain at scale is deferred, blocked on a ~$300 distillation step. The 21k number in this section will change from projection to measurement as that run lands. Do not cite the chain at scale until then.
One reversal: the chain’s worst-case 4-hop g_4 (max 206) is worse than dense’s (max 144). Walks usually lift deep chunks but occasionally misroute mass and bury one worse than dense would. This is a known RWR failure mode, not a metric artifact.
Graph construction is not free. If the corpus does not come with links, an entity layer has to be extracted. Wikis pay for themselves here, because the graph is a side effect of writing.

Deeper

title: Retrieval as trajectory
text:
Operator splitting
The RWR update \(\boldsymbol{\pi} = c \cdot \boldsymbol{\pi}_0 + (1 - c) \cdot \mathbf{P}^{\top} \boldsymbol{\pi}\) is a fixed-point iteration for a linear operator: a convex combination of the restart operator (which localizes) and the transition operator (which diffuses). This is a Douglas-Rachford-style split, and it lets you reason about which corpus properties affect which operator. Restart probability controls how far the mass travels from the seed before restarting; edge structure controls what the diffusion looks like when it does travel.
The practical upshot: tuning the retrieval is not a matter of picking a better embedding. It is a matter of adjusting the two operators. Restart up if the walk is drifting too far. Add edges if the mass is not reaching the neighborhoods where the evidence lives.
More on this in follow-up notes as the theory gets written up.
Practical takeaways

title: Retrieval as trajectory
section: What to do with all this
text:
What to do with all this

If your corpus is on the order of a thousand chunks and each question needs one chunk to answer, dense RAG is fine. You do not have an envelope problem.
If your corpus is on the order of ten thousand chunks and your questions cross-reference multiple documents, envelope assembly matters. Dense RAG will get first-hop evidence and miss most of the rest.
Wiki-style graphs are a cheap way to get envelope-assembly for free. If your team is already writing linked notes, the graph is already there. See the llm-wiki-memory-template for a scaffold.
GraphRAG-style community summaries (Edge et al., 2024) solve a related problem via hierarchical summarization instead of random walks. If your corpus is well-suited to Leiden communities, that path exists.
If the corpus does not come with links, plan for a graph-construction step. Concept extraction, entity linking, and typed-edge frontmatter all work; pick whichever fits your pipeline.

Learn more

title: Retrieval as trajectory
text:
Where to go from here

Data appendix: the rank tables, the gold-only comparison, the methodology.
The WGRA talk: full slides at chrissweet.github.io/wgra-talk-2026 if you want the visual walk-through.
The wiki-memory template: github.com/crcresearch/llm-wiki-memory-template for the pattern that makes graph construction a side effect of writing.
The Markov RAG source: github.com/tcflab/markov_embeddings_and_rag for the embedding-and-walk pipeline.

title: Retrieval as trajectory
text:

Liu, N. F., Lin, K., Hewitt, J., Paranjape, A., Bevilacqua, M., Petroni, F., & Liang, P. (2024). Lost in the middle: How language models use long contexts. Transactions of the Association for Computational Linguistics, 12, 157–173. https://doi.org/10.1162/tacl_a_00638


Trivedi, H., Balasubramanian, N., Khot, T., & Sabharwal, A. (2022). MuSiQue: Multihop questions via single-hop question composition. Transactions of the Association for Computational Linguistics, 10, 539–554. https://doi.org/10.1162/tacl_a_00475

title: Retrieval as trajectory
text:
Contact
Comments, corrections, and pointers to related work are welcome as issues on this repo, or by email to csweet1@nd.edu.
This site is versioned. When a claim changes, the page changes, and the commit history records why. It is meant as a living reference, not a frozen artifact.
